_meta.issue: #112-#118 [M15] Formative path S4 — Holak Scale (training module)
_meta.interaction: Decision classification (kind: classify) — formative exercise, NOT scored toward completion
_meta.source: Holak Scale maturity axes: output verifiability, decision reversibility. Attribution: quality-blog.eu/pl/blog/skala-holaka.
_meta.dataPolicy: ALL examples SYNTHETIC and generic (no real data/PII). Formative module — no pass threshold, no certificate.

intro: Mature AI work asks not only „can the model do it”, but „can the output be checked” and „can the decision be undone”. This module trains you to judge verifiability and reversibility before you trust an answer. It is a growth exercise, not a test.

screens.title: Two axes of trust: verifiability and reversibility

blocks.text: An output can be convincing in form yet wrong in substance. Before you use it, ask two questions: can I verify it against a source, and can a decision based on it be undone. The harder the verification and the lower the reversibility, the more human control is needed.

items: Verifiability — whether I have a way to confirm the answer is true.
Reversibility — whether the effects of the decision can be undone without major harm.
Audit trail — whether a record remains of what the decision was based on.

blocks.title: A Holak Scale axis
blocks.text: Designing work so AI-supported decisions stay checkable and reversible is a mark of reaching a higher level. More: quality-blog.eu/pl/blog/skala-holaka.

screens.title: When verification is enough, and when you need a human

blocks.text: Low risk: the output is easy to check and the decision easy to undo (e.g. a draft, a proposal for review). High risk: the effect is hard to reverse or hard to verify (e.g. sending a message to a customer, a permanent data change) — there a human approves.

blocks.title: The smooth-answer trap
blocks.text: The model's confident tone is not proof of correctness. No way to verify is a signal NOT to rely on the answer in a critical decision.

Next you will classify situations as low risk (verifiable output and reversible decision) or requiring human control. No pass threshold — the point is to build the reflex of asking about verification and reversibility.

interaction.title: Low risk or human control?
interaction.intro: Judge each situation. Feedback explains how verifiability and reversibility drive the need for control.

categories.label: Low risk (after verification)
categories.desc: Output easy to check, decision reversible.

items.text: AI proposes a draft note that you will review and edit anyway.
items.rationale: Easy verification and full reversibility — low risk.

items.text: AI generates content sent automatically to a customer without review.
items.rationale: Effect hard to undo — requires human approval.

items.text: AI suggests test cases that you compare against the requirement.
items.rationale: You can verify against the source before using.

items.text: AI decides to permanently delete records in a production database.
items.rationale: Irreversible and hard to verify — full human control.

items.text: AI summarizes a document while you have access to the original.
items.rationale: Source available → easy verification; the summary is reversible.

items.text: AI sets a candidate's score that nobody checks afterwards.
items.rationale: No verification + high impact → needs a human and an audit trail.

points: Before using an output, ask: can I verify this, and can the decision be undone.
The harder the verification and the lower the reversibility, the more human control.
The model's confident tone is not proof — keep a record of what the decision rested on.
checklist: Do I know how to check this answer against a source?
Can the effects of this decision be reversed?
Is an audit trail kept for the critical decision?
summary.checklistTitle: Checklist — before you trust an output
summary.nextStep: Next step: in one of your processes, add a verification step and the rule „irreversible decision = a human approves”.
